title: The substituted Class and the two-stage analysis
paras: Let's start with the substituted Class — and the two-stage analysis. Class Q grants planning permission for the change of use of certain agricultural and former agricultural buildings, with land within their curtilage, to Class C3 dwellinghouses. It covers, too, the building operations reasonably necessary to convert — and, since the 21st of May 2024, limited single-storey rear extensions. The Class exists to release redundant farm buildings for housing without testing the merits of countryside residential use against the development plan. And it is the most litigated and most technically demanding of the Part 3 rights. It generates a disproportionate share of prior approval appeals, and it rewards forensic preparation more than any other permitted development right — because eligibility turns on history, fact and structure, all of which must be evidenced rather than asserted.

The lineage matters, because it dates the learning. The right began as Class MB of the G-P-D-O 1995, from the 6th of April 2014. It was carried into the 2015 Order as Class Q, permitting three dwellings and 450 square metres, and it was enlarged in April 2018 — five dwellings within 465 square metres for larger dwellings, and 865 overall. Now, the current Class is not an amendment of that regime. It's a wholesale substitution. S-I 2024 number 579 replaced the entire Class from the 21st of May 2024. It retitled it — buildings on agricultural units and former agricultural buildings to dwellinghouses. It admitted former agricultural buildings. It recalibrated the limits to ten dwellings, 1,000 square metres cumulative, and 150 square metres per dwelling. It added the extension right. And it moved the reference date from the 20th of March 2013 to the 24th of July 2023. The transitional window preserving the old Class closed on the 20th of May 2025, so applications are now governed exclusively by the substituted Class — though pipeline appeals, and lawfulness questions on completed conversions, will meet the old regime for years.

Hold two bodies of learning apart. Pre-2024 case law and appeal decisions remain relevant only so far as the wording they construed survives. Much does — particularly the conversion concept — but the eligibility architecture is new, and older material on qualifying dates, dwelling mixes and floorspace must be discarded. An adviser still reasoning from the 20th of March 2013 date, or the larger and smaller dwelling split, is describing superseded law.

The permission is granted by Article 3(1) of the Order. Prior approval is a condition of that permission — not a separate permission. Keenan v Woking Borough Council, reported at 2017, E-W-C-A Civ 438, fixes the consequences. A completed Class Q conversion is development with planning permission — and the permission extends only to development falling squarely within the description of the Class and complying with its limitations and conditions. Prior approval, express or deemed, confers nothing on development outside the Class. Interpretation follows three working principles. First, the burden rests on the developer to demonstrate compliance with every element — no benefit of the doubt where the evidence leaves a requirement unproven. Second, the courts decline to gloss the Order with invented tests, while accepting its concepts carry content professionals can apply. And third, the whole of a development must fall within the Class. One element outside it, and nothing is permitted development.

Analysis therefore proceeds in two stages, and blurring them is the most common error on both sides of the desk. Eligibility asks whether the development is within the Class at all — site status and history, the Q.1 exclusions, the caps, the confined works, the space standard, the access requirement. These are hard-edged. There's no discretion, and failure on any one is fatal. Prior approval asks whether development within the Class is acceptable on the closed list of Q.2 matters — questions of planning judgment. Neither stage admits the development plan. Eligibility is governed by neither plan nor Framework, and on prior approval the Framework enters only through paragraph W(10)(b), so far as relevant to the subject matter. The P-P-G — including its well-known statement that the right does not extend to new structural elements — is support, never authority for a proposition the statutory words will not bear.

title: Qualifying buildings: the two gateways
paras: Now to qualifying buildings — and the two gateways. Since the 21st of May 2024 there are two distinct gateways, and identifying which applies is the first task on any instruction, because the eligibility conditions differ. The first gateway is a building that is part of an established agricultural unit. The site must have been part of the unit on the 24th of July 2023 — the date the amending proposals were announced, chosen for anti-avoidance — or, where it joined the unit later, part of it for at least ten years before development begins. That's Q.1(a). And note what is no longer required. The building need not have been used solely for agriculture, and need not be in agricultural use at the application date. Membership of the unit at the reference date is the test. A building on a working farm, used partly for storing non-agricultural items, is no longer disqualified — a marked liberalisation from the pre-2024 sole-use requirement.

The second gateway — the 2024 substitution's most significant enlargement — is the former agricultural building: one that was, but is no longer, part of an established agricultural unit. Q.1(b) imposes three cumulative conditions. First, the site was part of a unit on the 24th of July 2023. Second, where it ceased to be part of the unit after that date, at least ten years have passed since severance. And third — critically — since ceasing to be part of the unit, the site has not been used for any non-agricultural purpose. That third condition is absolute. Any intervening non-agricultural use, however minor or short-lived, disqualifies the building permanently. A severed barn used briefly for domestic storage or vehicle parking after leaving the holding fails the test — and nothing cures it.

Agricultural use has teeth of its own. Paragraph X defines an agricultural building as one used for agriculture for the purposes of a trade or business. That's a genuine filter — it excludes hobby farming, amenity grazing and buildings serving domestic paddocks, however agricultural they look. Agriculture itself takes its meaning from section 336 of the 1990 Act, and the recurring disputes are familiar. Equestrian use is not agriculture, save for grazing. Livery, kennels and rural storage businesses are not agriculture. And mixed uses require analysis of the planning unit, and of the character of the use as fact and degree. For buildings still within a unit, the battleground has shifted from sole use to unit membership at the reference date. For former buildings, use remains central — through the absolute intervening-use bar.

The subject of the right is a building, on the ordinary broad planning conception. But breadth of definition doesn't assist a structure so insubstantial that converting it would amount to reconstruction — that's Hibbitt territory. Part of a building can be converted, and a building can lawfully end up partly residential and partly agricultural; the Court of Appeal proceeded on that footing in Mansell. The state of the building at the application date is an eligibility fact. It must exist. It must be structurally capable of functioning as a dwelling with only the Q.1(j) works. And it must — excluding any proposed extension, but including proposed Q(c) operations — be capable of delivering space-standard-compliant dwellings. That's Q.1(o). A structural appraisal by a chartered engineer, addressed to capability rather than condition, is in practice indispensable — and its absence is a recurring cause of refusal.

Agricultural history is proved, not presumed. The core bundle: dated aerial and ground photography; farm business records, accounts and invoices demonstrating trade-or-business use; scheme records and holding numbers; tenancy and contracting documentation; and statutory declarations from those with direct knowledge, drafted with dates and specifics. Declarations are evidence, not incantations. Inspectors weigh them against the objective material, and discount the vague, the interested and the uncorroborated. Check the planning history too — conditions removing permitted development rights, agricultural occupancy conditions, and personal or temporary permissions bearing on the lawful use. And where the lawful use is genuinely contestable, the disciplined course is a certificate of lawfulness before the prior approval application.

Three further gates sit in Q.1, and they're routinely missed at appraisal. Where the site is occupied under an agricultural tenancy, the express consent of both landlord and tenant is required. Where an agricultural tenancy was terminated within the year before development, for the purpose of the development, the right is embargoed absent written agreement that the land is no longer needed for agriculture — that's Q.1(f). And where agricultural development under Class A(a) or B(a) of Part 6 — the new agricultural building rights — has been carried out on the established agricultural unit within the preceding ten years, Class Q is embargoed for the unit. That last one is the sting for the diversifying farm. The grain store erected under Part 6 in 2020 forecloses the barn conversion until 2030 — and the common-errors catalogue lists exactly this oversight.

title: The unit, the original limits and the caps
paras: Next, the unit, the original limits and the caps. The established agricultural unit is defined in paragraph X by reference to agricultural land occupied as a unit for the purposes of agriculture. It's an occupational concept, not a proprietary one, identified by the familiar planning unit reasoning — what land is occupied together for the purposes of agriculture? Ownership boundaries, title numbers and tenancy documents are evidence of occupation, but none is determinative. Land in separate ownership farmed as one holding can be a single unit, and contiguous land in common ownership farmed separately can be several. The unit question is where thin cases die. An authority faced with an asserted rather than proven unit is entitled to refuse, and inspectors routinely dismiss appeals on exactly that ground.

Three limits operate concurrently. No dwelling may exceed 150 square metres — that's Q.1(c). The development, together with all previous Class Q development, must not exceed ten dwellinghouses — Q.1(d)(i) — nor 1,000 square metres of cumulative floorspace — Q.1(d)(ii). The old larger and smaller architecture, with its 465 and 865 thresholds, is abolished. And the counting is done within the original limits of the established agricultural unit. Paragraph Q.3(2) fixes those limits as all the land comprising the unit at the time it came into existence — whether the unit ceased to exist before the 24th of July 2023, existed on that date, or comes into existence later.

The purpose is anti-avoidance, and it has sharp commercial edges. A holding cannot be subdivided, sold in parcels or reorganised to generate fresh allowances — the caps attach to the historic footprint of the unit. A purchaser of part acquires the Class Q history of the whole. So establishing what has previously been developed under Class Q, and under the former Classes MB and Q, across the original unit is a necessary head of due diligence on any rural land transaction — and an undocumented history is a priced risk, not a technicality. On the natural reading, previous Class Q development embraces development under the Class in its earlier forms. A unit that yielded five dwellings and 700 square metres before 2024 has five dwellings and 300 square metres of headroom left.

The arithmetic is straightforward, but the interactions are not. Ten dwellings at the numerical maximum require an average of 100 square metres each. Six dwellings at the individual maximum of 150 consume 900, leaving 100 square metres — enough for one further modest unit. The Order doesn't prescribe how floorspace is measured. Prevailing practice, supported by appeal decisions, treats it as internal floor area — but the point isn't settled by authority, and marginal schemes shouldn't be designed to the last square metre. And where a Q(b) extension is proposed, its floorspace counts towards the caps, while being excluded from the space-standard capability assessment.

Part of a building may be converted, leaving the remainder in agricultural or other lawful use — and a large building may be partially converted to stay within the caps. The scheme drawings must define the converted part precisely, because the boundary of the C3 use fixes both the floorspace calculation and the extent of the planning unit thereafter. Imprecision here stores up disputes about what was approved, and about what the resulting residential unit actually comprises.

title: Building operations, Hibbitt and the conversion boundary
paras: Now for building operations, Hibbitt, and the conversion boundary. Class Q(c) permits, alongside the change of use, building operations reasonably necessary to convert the building to C3 use, or to extend it. And Q.1(j) confines those operations to two categories. First, the installation or replacement of windows, doors, roofs or exterior walls, and of water, drainage, electricity, gas or other services — in each case to the extent reasonably necessary for the building to function as a dwellinghouse. And second, partial demolition to the extent reasonably necessary to carry out those works. The drafting repays close reading. The list is exhaustive — works outside it, however sensible, are outside the Class. Reasonably necessary qualifies twice over: the works must be of the listed kinds, and must go no further than the conversion requires. Partial demolition is parasitic on the permitted works — freestanding demolition, and demolition amounting to substantial clearance, is not permitted. The P-P-G adds that the right does not extend to the construction of new structural elements. That's consistent with the statutory words. If the existing structure can't carry the conversion without a new frame, new load-bearing walls or comprehensive rebuilding, the works are not conversion at all.

Hibbitt v the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2016, E-W-H-C 2853, Admin, is the case the whole field turns on. A steel-framed building at Wysall, largely open on three sides with limited cladding. Prior approval refused. The inspector concluded the works needed to make it a dwelling went beyond conversion, and the High Court dismissed the challenge. Class Q permits a conversion — but not works of such magnitude that, in practical reality, what is being undertaken is a rebuild or a fresh build. The absence of a statutory definition of conversion did not deprive the concept of content — the Order is addressed to a professional audience able to apply the term — and where the line falls in a given case is planning judgment, reviewable only on public law grounds. The ratio, shortly stated: the starting point must be a building genuinely capable of conversion, and works amounting in substance to reconstruction fall outside the Class — even where each individual operation, viewed in isolation, appears on the Q.1(j) list.

Hibbitt survives the 2024 substitution in full. Q(c) retains the operative concept, and Q.1(j) reproduces the confined list. If anything, the substituted Class reinforces it, because the space-standard capability test and the dimensional controls both presuppose an existing building of substance. The assessment is holistic. The decision-maker looks at the totality of the works, and asks what, in practical reality, is being undertaken. Open-sided, skeletal and dilapidated structures are poor candidates. And understating the works to pass prior approval is self-defeating, because development carried out beyond the permitted operations is unlawful and unprotected — whatever the approval says.

The envelope is controlled by Q.1(h). No development may result in the external dimensions extending beyond those of the existing building at any given point, subject to two exceptions — the Q(b) extension, and protrusions of up to 0.2 metres to accommodate permitted building operations. That tolerance resolved a long-running practical difficulty, admitting external wall insulation, cladding and roof build-ups that marginally enlarge the envelope. But it's a tolerance, not an allowance for design ambition. The Q(b) extension right is tightly drawn by Q.1(i). Single storey. Rear only. No more than 4 metres beyond the rear wall. Eaves no higher than the existing eaves. No higher than the lower of the existing roof or 4 metres. Not beyond a side or principal elevation. And only on land already covered by a hard surface — one provided on or before the 24th of July 2023, or in place for ten years. The hard-surface condition is an eligibility fact requiring dated evidence. And the extension can't be used to achieve space-standard compliance, because Q.1(o) tests the existing building excluding it.

The structural report is where the conversion boundary is evidenced. It should address the frame, foundations, walls and roof against the loads and works proposed, and say in terms whether the building can be converted without works beyond Q.1(j). Reports that survey condition without addressing capability, or that reveal the need for underpinning, new foundations or frame replacement, invite refusal. And where the engineer's honest answer is that substantial reconstruction is needed, the correct advice is a planning application — not a Class Q application.

title: Prior approval: the matters
paras: On to prior approval — the matters. Paragraph Q.2 attaches the prior approval condition in a form that varies with the species of development, and identifying the correct matter set at the outset isn't pedantry — an application framed against the wrong list invites validation disputes and jurisdictional appeals. For Q(a) with Q(c) building operations — the great majority — there are seven matters: transport and highways impacts; noise impacts; contamination risks on the site; flooding risks on the site; whether the location or siting of the building makes it otherwise impractical or undesirable for the use to change; the design or external appearance of the building; and adequate natural light in all habitable rooms. For a pure Q(a) change of use, design and external appearance drops away, leaving six. And where a Q(b) extension is included, the impact of the extension on the amenity of adjoining premises is added. Refusal on any single matter defeats the application. And eligibility is logically prior — an authority that considers the development outside the Class refuses under W(3), and applicants should expect eligibility to be tested at this stage, even though it isn't itself a prior approval matter.

Transport and highways, in the Class Q context, means the safety and adequacy of the access, visibility splays, the capacity of rural lanes to absorb domestic traffic, and refuse and servicing — with the highway authority's view usually framing the debate. Keep it distinct from the separate eligibility requirement in Q.1(p), that the building must have suitable existing access to a public highway. That requirement is absolute, and it isn't cured by proposing access improvements — the prior approval matter then assesses the impacts of using the access that exists. The common mistake is conflating the two, and offering engineering works to create an access where none suitably exists. Noise runs in both directions on a farm. The assessment is of impacts on intended occupiers — grain driers, livestock buildings, machinery — but a scheme placing dwellings hard against an active yard invites refusal here, or under location and siting. And survey data must cover representative operating periods, including seasonal and night-time activity, with mitigation that doesn't depend on permanently closed windows.

Contamination reflects the characteristic agricultural risks: fuel and oil storage, sheep dip, pesticide and fertiliser residues, made ground — and asbestos cement roofing, ubiquitous in the stock, whose management belongs in the application rather than in post-approval surprise. A phase 1 assessment is the minimum, with intrusive investigation where plausible pathways to future residents emerge. Flooding is directed at the safety of the residential occupiers: a site-specific flood risk assessment in the higher-risk zones, the Environment Agency consulted, and the substance of national flood policy applied through the Framework gateway. Finished floor levels, resilience, and safe access and egress are the working currency — not sequential site selection across the district.

Location and siting — whether the location or siting of the building makes it otherwise impractical or undesirable for the building to change to residential use — is unique to Class Q, and it's the most contested matter. The P-P-G directs the ordinary meaning: impractical means not sensible or realistic; undesirable means harmful or objectionable. It is expressly not an opportunity to apply development plan tests, or a general sustainability assessment — a building may be approved in a location where a planning application for a new dwelling would fail. The paradigm refusals: buildings in the working heart of an operational farmyard; buildings surrounded by intensive livestock units, with the attendant odour and noise; buildings in physically hazardous or wholly inaccessible locations. Isolation, without more, is a weak ground. The common authority error is dressing plan-based countryside protection as undesirability. The common applicant error is ignoring the realities of a working farm wrapped around the appeal building.

Design and external appearance — engaged only where Q(c) operations are proposed — examines the building as converted: fenestration pattern, materials, domestic paraphernalia, and the retention of agricultural character. It isn't a gateway to redesigning the building into a conventional house, and it equally disciplines schemes that would domesticate the building beyond recognition. Elevations should be complete and honest, including flues, rooflights and services. Natural light requires the authority to be satisfied that every habitable room receives adequate light, on floor plans that must accompany the application, showing window positions and dimensions. Deep-plan barns with limited openings are the difficult cases, and the matter interacts with Q.1(h) — new openings are permitted works, but the envelope can't grow to borrow light. Refusals commonly follow plans without window dimensions, or bedrooms lit only from rooflights of doubtful adequacy. And the extension amenity matter — outlook, overbearing effect, light, privacy for adjoining premises — is kept modest in practice by the constrained dimensions of what Q(b) permits.

title: Procedure, the 56 days and the space standard
paras: Now, procedure — the 56 days, and the space standard. Paragraph W governs the procedure. The application must include a written description of the proposed development — which for Class Q must state the works relied on under Q(c) — a plan indicating the site and showing the development, floor plans addressing natural light, contact details, and the fee. The authority may refuse under W(3) where it considers the development doesn't comply with the Class, or the information is insufficient to establish compliance or to assess the matters. A W(3) refusal is a refusal of prior approval, carrying the ordinary right of appeal. Authorities use it legitimately to determine eligibility disputes; they misuse it as a device for demanding material beyond the statutory requirements. Notice must be given to adjoining owners and occupiers, or by site display. The relevant consultees must be engaged — the highway authority, and the Environment Agency in flood cases — and representations taken into account so far as they bear on the listed matters.

Development must not begin before written notice of the determination, or the expiry of 56 days from receipt — whichever is earlier. Silence for 56 days permits the developer to proceed in accordance with the details provided. The period may be extended, but only by agreement in writing — and that point was genuinely contested. Warren Farm held that Article 7 did not permit extension for Part 3 applications, but the Court of Appeal in Gluck, reported at 2020, E-W-C-A Civ 1756, put the matter beyond doubt the other way. No formal instrument is required — an email exchange showing proposal and assent suffices, construed objectively. The practical disciplines follow. Diarise day 56. Treat any request for more time as a strategic decision rather than a courtesy — agreeing surrenders the deemed consent; refusing usually produces a refusal on the papers. And record the position in unambiguous terms.

Deemed consent remains subject to Keenan. Proceeding on silence protects only development genuinely within the Class — and on Class Q the eligibility questions are numerous and factual: the gateway, the unit history, the caps, the works, the access, the space standard. Where any is arguable, the prudent sequence is a section 192 certificate of lawfulness before works begin, because neither a grant of prior approval nor the expiry of the period carries a presumption of lawfulness. Refusal carries a section 78 appeal, normally by written representations, confined to the statutory matters — and, where raised, eligibility.

Two conditions attach to the development. It must be carried out in accordance with the details approved — or, on deemed consent, the details applied for. That's W(12). And it must be completed — not commenced — within three years of the prior approval date. That's Q.2. Completion is the statutory requirement. A conversion begun but unfinished at year three isn't saved by the doctrine of lawful commencement that attends ordinary permissions, and works continuing beyond the deadline proceed without permission. Realistic construction programmes belong in the decision whether to apply at all — and the completion date deserves the same diary discipline as day 56.

The nationally described space standard enters twice, and the double import is deliberate. Q.1(o) makes it an eligibility requirement: development is not permitted unless the existing building — excluding any proposed Q(b) extension, but including proposed Q(c) operations — would be capable of complying with the standard. That's the standard of the 27th of March 2015, read with the 2016 notes. Article 3(9A) separately withholds permission for any dwelling under 37 square metres gross internal area, or failing the standard. Three implications follow. First, small buildings are simply ineligible — and they can't be brought within the Class by extending them. Second, unit layouts must be designed to the standard from the outset: gross internal area, bedroom sizes, storage, floor-to-ceiling height, all against the published tables. And third, the standard polices subdivision ambition, because carving out the maximum number of units founders where individual units fall below it — whatever headroom remains under the 1,000 square metre cap. Apply the current published text, not memory.

title: Enforcement, Mansell and the fallback
paras: Finally, enforcement, Mansell and the fallback. The development authorised is the development described and approved, and paragraph W(12) ties implementation to those details. Dharmeshkumar, reported at 2025, E-W-H-C 2513, Admin — decided on a Class O conversion, but construing the paragraph W code that governs Class Q equally — establishes two propositions. First, the approved drawings are incorporated by operation of W(12)(a) itself, without incorporating words on the decision notice, and ambiguity about what was approved may be resolved from extrinsic material, including the application drawings. Second, under Article 3(5), unlawful operations carried out as part of the development deprive the change of use of the Order's protection. A conversion built otherwise than in accordance with the approved details, or with works exceeding Q.1(j), isn't a lawful development with defects — it's development without planning permission. And there's no section 73 or section 96A route for amending a prior approval. A scheme that changes after approval requires a fresh prior approval, or a planning application for the changed element — secured before the works are undertaken.

The enforcement time limit is ten years for breaches occurring on or after the 25th of April 2024. The former four-year period, for operational development and for change of use to a single dwellinghouse, survives only for earlier breaches — so historic conversions must be dated before advising on immunity, with the concealment regimes noted separately. Certificates do the two jobs they always do. Section 191 establishes the lawfulness of what has been done. Section 192, sought before development, remains the only instrument that conclusively establishes that a proposed scheme is lawful. And a Class Q consent settles the planning position — and nothing else. Building Regulations approval is required, and the structural, thermal, fire and drainage upgrades must be achievable within the Q.1(j) works. Where they're not, the scheme fails Hibbitt, rather than merely failing building control. Barns are classic bat roosts and barn owl sites — ecological survey in the correct season is in practice essential, and a European protected species licence may be needed before works, whatever the G-P-D-O says. Foul drainage in unsewered locations engages the permitting and general binding rules regime. Access works need highway consents. And private rights — restrictive covenants, rights of way, drainage easements, wayleaves — have defeated more than one consented conversion, and belong on every due diligence list.

Mansell v Tonbridge and Malling Borough Council, reported at 2017, E-W-C-A Civ 1314, supplies the strategic dimension. A council granted permission for four dwellings contrary to countryside policy, giving weight to the fallback that the barn could be converted under Class Q into what the officer regarded as a contrived and piecemeal form of development. The High Court quashed the permission; the Court of Appeal restored it. Lord Justice Lindblom confirmed the orthodox approach: a fallback is a material consideration where there's a real prospect of implementation — a low threshold, requiring neither probability nor certainty — and the weight it carries is planning judgment for the decision-maker. The court also proceeded on the footing that partial conversion is within the Class, and that the caps bite on the development rather than the whole building — propositions that survive the 2024 rewording, even though the numerical content is superseded.

Mansell's enduring importance is as a bargaining asset. Authorities may lawfully grant permission for development conflicting with the plan where the realistic alternative is an inferior permitted development outcome — and the enlarged 2024 Class, a fallback of up to ten dwellings rather than five, strengthens the tactic. The discipline is credibility. The fallback must be real: an eligible building, the evidence assembled, preferably a prior approval secured — or at least a robust appraisal demonstrating that approval would be obtained. Inspectors and courts are alert to fallbacks constructed for negotiation, and an authority is entitled to give little weight to a Class Q position that would founder on Hibbitt, on the unit history, or on the Q.1 exclusions.

The errors met in practice cluster predictably, and the catalogue is the audit list. On eligibility: pre-2024 learning; the wrong gateway; intervening non-agricultural use missed on former buildings; unit histories asserted rather than evidenced; Part 6 Class A(a) or B(a) development within ten years overlooked; and article 2(3) land checked for the building, but not the site. On the works: rebuilds in conversion clothing; condition reports that never address capability; demolition beyond what the permitted operations require; envelopes beyond the 0.2 metre tolerance. On procedure: no window dimensions; evidence commissioned after validation; extensions agreed by loose correspondence; deemed consent relied on where eligibility was arguable. On implementation: drift from the approved drawings; external works without their separate permission; and the three-year completion missed. Every one of them is avoidable by the same discipline — Class Q treated as a statutory eligibility exercise, evidenced to certificate standard.
